title: Recognizing the 4th anniversary of the Trump administration's Secretary of the Air Force announcing Redstone Arsenal in Huntsville, Alabama, as the preferred location for United States Space Command Headquarters.
stage_label: Referred to the House Committee on Armed Services.
plain_summary: This resolution celebrates the four-year anniversary of when the Air Force chose Huntsville, Alabama as the best place for the US Space Command Headquarters. The resolution says that Huntsville was picked fairly through a proper selection process during the Trump administration. However, President Biden later changed the decision and chose Colorado Springs, Colorado instead. The resolution criticizes this change and wants the new Trump administration to switch the headquarters back to Huntsville, Alabama.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Armed Services.

sections.title: Resolution Title
sections.text: This resolution recognizes the fourth anniversary of when the Trump administration's Secretary of the Air Force announced that Redstone Arsenal in Huntsville, Alabama, would be the preferred location for the United States Space Command Headquarters.

sections.title: Background Findings
sections.text: On January thirteenth, two thousand twenty one, the United States Air Force announced that Redstone Arsenal in Huntsville, Alabama, would be the preferred location for United States Space Command Headquarters. January thirteenth, two thousand twenty five, marks the fourth anniversary of this decision that was announced during Donald Trump's first presidency. President Trump directed the Secretary of Defense to bring back United States Space Command as a unified combatant command (a major military organization) on December eighteenth, two thousand eighteen. Because of President Trump's direction, the Secretary of Defense told the United States Air Force Basing Office to start a process to find the best permanent location for United States Space Command headquarters. This process is called the two thousand nineteen Basing Action. The Secretary of the Air Force signed a memo approving a temporary headquarters while waiting to select a preferred permanent location for United States Space Command on January fifteenth, two thousand twenty. On April fifteenth, two thousand nineteen, the Secretary of the Air Force was given the authority to make a decision on the preferred permanent location for United States Space Command Headquarters. Following President Trump's direction, the Secretary of Defense officially brought back United States Space Command as a unified combatant command on August twenty ninth, two thousand nineteen. Because Congress had concerns about the two thousand nineteen Basing Action, the Secretary of Defense and Secretary of the Air Force met to discuss a changed process. They approved the two thousand twenty Basing Action, called the Strategic Basing Action, on April twenty seventh, two thousand twenty.

sections.title: Selection Process
sections.text: The approved Strategic Basing Action had three phases and started on May fourteenth, two thousand twenty, beginning with the Self Nomination Phase. During this phase, military installations that were interested in hosting United States Space Command Headquarters could submit proposals. During the Self Nomination Phase, sixty six possible locations in twenty six states were nominated. The Air Force Basing Office determined that fifty locations met the initial requirements. The second Strategic Basing Action phase, called the Evaluation Phase, began on July twenty third, two thousand twenty. This phase looked at each of the fifty locations based on four evaluation factors called Mission, Capacity, Community, and Costs to the Department of Defense, with twenty one criteria between the factors. The Evaluation Phase ended on November eighteenth, two thousand twenty. The United States Air Force publicly announced six finalists to advance to the third phase of the Strategic Basing Action on November nineteenth, two thousand twenty. The finalists were ranked in this order: first, Redstone Arsenal in Huntsville, Alabama; second, Offutt Air Force Base in Bellevue, Nebraska; third, Joint Base San Antonio in San Antonio, Texas; fourth, Peterson Air Force Base in Colorado Springs, Colorado; fifth, Kirtland Air Force Base in Albuquerque, New Mexico; and sixth, Patrick Air Force Base in Cape Canaveral, Florida. A two thousand twenty two Department of Defense Inspector General report titled Evaluation of the Air Force Selection Process for the Permanent Location of the U.S. Space Command Headquarters found that there was a large gap in qualification that occurred after the top two locations during the Evaluation Phase.

sections.title: Final Selection and Findings
sections.text: The third phase of the Strategic Basing Action, called the Selection Phase, was conducted from December fourth, two thousand twenty, through January seventh, two thousand twenty one. The findings of the Selection Phase resulted in the six finalists from the Evaluation Phase being ranked in this order: first, Redstone Arsenal in Huntsville, Alabama; second, Kirtland Air Force Base in Albuquerque, New Mexico; third, Offutt Air Force Base in Bellevue, Nebraska; fourth, Joint Base San Antonio in San Antonio, Texas; fifth, Peterson Air Force Base in Colorado Springs, Colorado; and sixth, Patrick Air Force Base in Cape Canaveral, Florida. The Strategic Basing Action found that Redstone Arsenal in Huntsville, Alabama, consistently ranked as the top location throughout the process and compared more favorably across the four key factors and twenty one criteria than any other finalist location. The Strategic Basing Action found that Huntsville, more so than any other finalist location, provided a large qualified workforce, quality schools, superior infrastructure, and low initial and recurring costs to the Department of Defense. These points proved that Redstone Arsenal in Huntsville, Alabama, was and remains the best location for United States Space Command Headquarters. This information guided the decision of the United States Air Force selecting the site as the preferred location on January thirteenth, two thousand twenty one. The findings of the Strategic Basing Action have been supported by reports following later Department of Defense Inspector General and Government Accountability Office investigations. Despite an extensive process determining that Redstone Arsenal in Huntsville, Alabama, was the best possible location for United States Space Command, on July thirty first, two thousand twenty three, President Joseph Biden and his administration chose to disregard the findings of the Strategic Basing Action and announced the intention to locate the headquarters at the fifth best location, Colorado Springs, Colorado.

sections.title: House Resolution
sections.text: Therefore, the House of Representatives recognizes that the United States Air Force's Strategic Basing Action process followed law and policy and was justified in identifying Huntsville as the preferred permanent location for United States Space Command Headquarters. The House strongly commends President Donald Trump and his first administration for completing a strong and fact based Strategic Basing Action focused on what was best to ensure the national security and fiscal responsibility of the United States and well being of service members and their families. The House strongly condemns President Joseph Biden and his administration for disregarding the findings of the Strategic Basing Action and allowing United States Space Command Headquarters to be based at the fifth best location. The House encourages President Donald Trump and his incoming second administration to halt the Biden administration's decision and immediately proceed in establishing a permanent headquarters for United States Space Command at Redstone Arsenal in Huntsville, Alabama.
